concepts.title: Directing as a Function of Management
concepts.description: Directing is one of the four basic functions of management and is concerned with the process of influencing the behavior of subordinates to achieve organizational goals.

concepts.title: Role of a Director
concepts.description: A director's role includes planning, organizing, staffing, directing, controlling, and problem-solving. They must also motivate employees, set goals, and allocate resources effectively.

concepts.title: Three Main Steps of Directing
concepts.description: The three main steps of directing are: (1) Communicating the organization's goals and objectives to subordinates, (2) Issuing instructions and orders to achieve these goals, and (3) Providing feedback and guidance to ensure that the work is done properly.

concepts.title: Over-Direction vs. Under-Direction
concepts.description: Students often get confused between over-direction and under-direction. Over-direction occurs when a director micromanages and interferes too much with the work of subordinates, while under-direction occurs when a director provides too little guidance and support. The ideal approach is to strike a balance between the two.

concepts.title: Key Characteristics of Effective Directing
concepts.description: Effective directing involves being clear, specific, and consistent in communication, providing feedback and guidance, and motivating employees to achieve organizational goals.

concepts.title: SMART Goals
concepts.description: To remember the characteristics of SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound), use the mnemonic SMART: 'Some Managers Always Reach Targets'.

concepts.title: Purpose of Directing
concepts.description: The primary purpose of directing is to achieve organizational objectives by influencing the behavior of subordinates.

concepts.title: Methods of Directing
concepts.description: There are three main methods of directing: Autocratic, Democratic, and Laissez-Faire. Autocratic directing involves giving orders, democratic directing involves seeking suggestions, and laissez-faire directing involves giving employees a lot of freedom.
